slides.title: Workload Shift
slides.takeaway: Agentic applications mix dependency-critical agents with long tool stalls, so KV cache becomes a shared workflow resource rather than a per-request detail.

evidence.detail: Representative multi-agent workloads combine frequent tool calls with internal agent dependencies.

boxes.label: Workloads
boxes.body: Code-Writer stresses many specialized agents and frequent file or tool calls; Deep Research uses fewer agents but a tighter dependency chain.

boxes.label: Systems implication
boxes.body: Some agents sit on the critical path while others wait on external tools, so naive KV-cache placement wastes scarce GPU memory.

qa_expectations: Figure 1 stays readable at large scale.
Both support boxes stay to two short lines.

slides.title: Two Cache Failures
slides.takeaway: Tokencake starts from two coupled pathologies: critical agents can be evicted by less important work, and stalled agents leave idle KV cache on the GPU.

evidence.detail: Stalled agents can occupy 18.5% of used KV cache at peak.

boxes.label: Space
boxes.body: Critical inversion forces recomputation when a non-critical agent occupies scarce GPU blocks first.

boxes.label: Time
boxes.body: Tool stalls leave the first inference prefix idle on GPU; peaks reach 18.5% of used KV cache.

qa_expectations: Both composite figures remain legible side by side.
The support text does not push the figures into thumbnail size.

slides.title: Tokencake Thesis
slides.takeaway: Tokencake co-optimizes KV cache across both space and time instead of separating workflow scheduling from memory management.

evidence.detail: One graph feeds both schedulers, and coordination keeps their policies consistent.

boxes.label: Core idea
boxes.body: Tokencake couples graph-aware metadata with two policies: proactive offload and prefetch around tool stalls, and critical-agent reservation under memory pressure.

qa_expectations: The overview figure stays readable in the side column.

slides.title: Time Scheduler
slides.takeaway: Function-call stalls are predictable enough to turn idle KV-cache time into a planned offload window.

evidence.detail: Tokencake is proactive and event-driven, unlike reactive offload policies.

boxes.label: Trigger
boxes.body: Tokencake uses function-call events rather than generic inactivity heuristics, so the offload window is explicit.

boxes.label: Mechanism
boxes.body: Static graph analysis gives cold-start estimates, runtime feedback refines them, and predictive upload hides transfer latency before the agent resumes.

qa_expectations: The lifecycle figure stays readable at near-full width.

slides.title: Space Scheduler
slides.takeaway: Critical-path agents need explicit memory protection, so Tokencake reserves GPU KV blocks by hybrid priority rather than arrival order.

evidence.detail: Priority and pressure jointly control the reserved pool.

boxes.label: Priority
boxes.body: Critical agents are chosen by hybrid priority, combining DAG structure with runtime importance.

boxes.label: Reservation
boxes.body: The reserved pool grows with memory pressure and is divided across critical agents by score and historical memory demand.

bullets: The configurable critical_ratio controls how many agent types receive protection.
qa_expectations: Figure 8 remains legible without shrinking the support text.

slides.title: Evaluation Setup
slides.takeaway: The evaluation isolates Tokencake's memory-policy contribution on two representative multi-agent applications under increasing load.

evidence.detail: Code-Writer and Deep Research stress different multi-agent pathologies.
Baselines do not include Tokencake's proactive offload or predictive upload.

boxes.label: Workloads
boxes.body: Code-Writer stresses concurrency and tool use; Deep Research stresses dependency depth and inter-agent coordination.

boxes.label: Baselines
boxes.body: vLLM is the main baseline, while LightLLM shows Tokencake still wins against another optimized serving stack.

boxes.label: Metrics
boxes.body: The paper reports end-to-end latency, GPU KV utilization, abnormal agents, and offload microbenchmarks.

bullets: Request arrivals follow a Poisson process across increasing QPS.
qa_expectations: The slide stays compact without needing an extra workload figure.

slides.title: Loaded-System Gains
slides.takeaway: Tokencake's advantage grows with load because it frees GPU memory for active requests instead of letting stalled agents pin it.

evidence.detail: At 1.0 QPS, Tokencake reduces end-to-end latency by over 47.06% versus vLLM.

boxes.label: Main result
boxes.body: At 1.0 QPS, Tokencake cuts end-to-end latency by over 47.06% versus vLLM, and the gap widens as memory pressure rises.

boxes.label: Interpretation
boxes.body: The benefit is modest when the system is not memory-bound, but it grows once tool stalls and agent interference start to constrain batch size.

qa_expectations: The multi-panel latency figure stays readable at full width.

slides.title: Utilization and Stability
slides.takeaway: Tokencake's latency gain comes from repurposing memory for active work and reducing contention-driven outliers on the critical path.

evidence.detail: Utilization reaches 86-87%, up to 16.9% above vLLM, while abnormal agents are those above 1.5x the type average.

boxes.label: Memory efficiency
boxes.body: Tokencake keeps GPU KV utilization at 86-87%, up to 16.9% above vLLM, because idle caches leave the GPU.

boxes.label: Critical path
boxes.body: Abnormal agents above 1.5x the type-average latency drop sharply, which signals fewer contention-induced stalls.

qa_expectations: Both evaluation plots remain readable when stacked.

slides.title: Offload Beats Recompute
slides.takeaway: Offloading only helps if transfer stays far cheaper than recomputation, and Tokencake's buffering and reservation optimizations make that true in practice.

evidence.detail: 4096 blocks transfer in about 60 ms versus nearly 9,000 ms recomputation; 5120-block upload drops from 15,163 ms to 4.4 ms.

boxes.label: Transfer versus recompute
boxes.body: For 4096 blocks, transfer takes about 60 ms while recomputation takes nearly 9,000 ms, so reuse is decisively cheaper.

boxes.label: System cost
boxes.body: Tokencake cuts 5120-block upload latency from 15,163 ms to 4.4 ms, making proactive offload operationally viable.

qa_expectations: The two tradeoff plots stay readable when stacked.

slides.title: What This Paper Establishes
slides.takeaway: Tokencake's real contribution is memory-aware, agent-aware co-design: once KV cache is managed as a workflow resource, agentic serving can trade idle memory for lower latency and fewer critical-path stalls.

boxes.label: Main lesson
boxes.body: The paper is strongest when it treats KV cache as a first-class shared resource across both time and space.

boxes.label: Why it convinces
boxes.body: The mechanism slides connect cleanly to workload-level latency gains, higher utilization, and fewer critical-path outliers.

boxes.label: Limits
boxes.body: The predictor is simple and the evaluation is single-GPU, so multi-GPU coordination and richer forecasts remain open problems.

qa_expectations: The final slide reads as a judgment, not a section-by-section recap.

notes: Deck arc follows the default systems paper reading structure: workload shift, failure modes, design thesis, the two schedulers, evaluation setup, main results, supporting tradeoff evidence, and critique.
